preFix.title: Fitting tiles around a window reveal
preFix.subtitle: Cut and bed tiles on the window splay and return
preFix.excerpt: How to tile the internal reveal of a window: marking and cutting the tiles to fit the splay and return, finishing the exposed tile edges with edge trim or a mitred joint, and grouting the reveal to match the wall.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Wall and Floor Tiling syllabus.

content.text: Tiling a
window reveal

is one of the more involved cuts in a tiling job. The reveals are typically narrower than a full tile and require careful measuring, clean cuts, and a decision on how to finish the exposed edge where the reveal tile meets the window frame.
When to use it
When tiling a kitchen or bathroom wall that includes a window. The technique applies equally to a full window reveal and to a partial one where only the sill or side returns need tiling.
What you need
Tiles from the same batch as the main wall, a tile cutter or angle grinder with a diamond disc,
edge trim
in the matching size, flexible tile adhesive, tile spacers, and grout. The reveal must be square and flat; if the plaster is damaged, repair and allow to cure before tiling.
Step-by-step
Step 1, finish the main wall tiles to the reveal edge.

Tile the main wall face first, allowing the edge of the last tile on each side of the reveal to sit at the reveal face. If you are using edge trim, bed the trim into the adhesive along the reveal edge before placing the main wall tile, so the trim's return lip sits against the reveal plaster.
Step 2, measure the reveal depth.

Measure the depth of the reveal from the main wall face to the window frame at several points; reveals in older houses are rarely parallel. Cut the reveal tiles to this depth, allowing for a 2 mm gap at the window frame that will be sealed with flexible silicone sealant rather than grout.
Step 3, bed the reveal tiles.

Apply adhesive to the reveal surface and to the back of each tile (back-butter on narrow reveals where the notched trowel cannot be used). Place tiles from the head (top) of the reveal downward, using spacers to maintain the joint. The outer edge of each reveal tile butts against the main wall tile; the inner edge faces the window frame and should be the cut edge, hidden by the silicone line.
Step 4, grout and seal.

Grout the reveal joints to match the main wall. Seal the joint at the window frame with a matching or complementary flexible silicone sealant. This joint must flex; a rigid grout will crack as the frame and tiles expand and contract with temperature changes.
What it looks like when done right

The reveal tiles continue the main wall pattern into the reveal. The edge trim (if used) gives a clean polished edge at the face of the wall. The silicone joint at the window frame is straight, even in width, and matches or complements the grout colour.
Common mistakes
Grouting the frame joint.
Grout at the window frame will crack within the first season as the frame moves. Always seal this joint with flexible silicone.
Cutting the reveal tiles too wide.
Tiles cut tight to the window frame leave no room for the silicone bead. Leave at least 2 mm clearance to the frame.
